title: Mastering Command Arguments
description: Learn step-by-step how to handle command arguments with full explanations, structure, validation, and real-world usage.

lessons.title: Argument Basics
lessons.content:
# Argument Basics (FULL GUIDE)

## Step 1: Where This Code Goes

Open IntelliJ
→ Go to your command class
→ Find your onCommand method

Example structure:

```java
public boolean onCommand(CommandSender sender, Command cmd, String label, String[] args) {

}
```

You will write ALL argument code INSIDE this method.

## Step 2: What Are Arguments?

Arguments = words after the command

Example:
/heal Steve

- args[0] = "Steve"

## Step 3: Code Example

```java
if (args.length > 0) {
    sender.sendMessage("Hello " + args[0]);
}
```

## Step 4: FULL Breakdown

args
→ This is an ARRAY (list of values)

args.length
→ how many arguments were typed

args[0]
→ first argument

+ → joins text together

## Step 5: VERY IMPORTANT (ARRAY RULE)

Arrays start at index 0

args[0] = first
args[1] = second
args[2] = third

## Step 6: What Happens In-Game

Command:
/hello Steve

Output:
Hello Steve

## Step 7: CRITICAL ERROR

❌ Using args[0] without checking length
→ Causes ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException

## Step 8: Why This Happens

If player types:
/hello

There is NO args[0]

## Mini Challenge
Make it say "Welcome <name>"

## Recap
- args = list
- starts at 0
- MUST check length

quiz.question: What happens if args empty and accessed?

lessons.title: Validating Arguments
lessons.content:
# Argument Validation (FULL GUIDE)

## Step 1: Why Validation Matters

Players can type ANYTHING:
- Missing args
- Too many args
- Wrong format

You MUST control this.

## Step 2: Basic Length Check

```java
if (args.length < 1) {
    sender.sendMessage("Usage: /heal <player>");
    return true;
}
```

## Step 3: Breakdown

args.length < 1
→ no arguments provided

return true;
→ STOPS command execution

## Step 4: Exact Length Check

```java
if (args.length != 1) {
    sender.sendMessage("Invalid usage");
    return true;
}
```

!= means NOT equal

## Step 5: Where This Goes

ALWAYS at the TOP of your command method

## Step 6: What Happens In-Game

Wrong usage:
/heal

Output:
Usage: /heal <player>

## Step 7: Beginner Mistake

❌ Checking AFTER using args
✔️ ALWAYS check BEFORE

## Mini Challenge
Require exactly 2 arguments

## Recap
Validate FIRST → then use args

lessons.title: Player Arguments
lessons.content:
# Player Arguments (FULL GUIDE)

## Step 1: Goal

Convert text → Player object

Example:
/heal Steve

## Step 2: Code

```java
Player target = Bukkit.getPlayer(args[0]);

if (target == null) {
    sender.sendMessage("Player not found");
    return true;
}
```

## Step 3: FULL Breakdown

Bukkit.getPlayer(...)
→ Finds online player

args[0]
→ player name

== null
→ means player NOT found

## Step 4: WHY null happens

- Player offline
- Name wrong

## Step 5: Where This Goes

AFTER validation check

## Step 6: What Happens

If player exists → continue
If not → message shown

## Step 7: CRITICAL RULE

❌ Using target without null check → crash

## Mini Challenge
Send target a message

## Recap
String → Player
Check null ALWAYS

lessons.title: Number Arguments
lessons.content:
# Number Arguments (FULL GUIDE)

## Step 1: Problem

args are ALWAYS strings

You must convert them

## Step 2: Code

```java
int amount;

try {
    amount = Integer.parseInt(args[0]);
} catch (NumberFormatException e) {
    sender.sendMessage("Must be a number!");
    return true;
}
```

## Step 3: FULL Breakdown

Integer.parseInt(...)
→ converts string → int

try { }
→ attempts risky code

catch (...) { }
→ runs if error happens

## Step 4: Why This Is Needed

If player types:
/give abc

→ CRASH without try-catch

## Step 5: What Happens

Valid number → works
Invalid → message

## Step 6: Beginner Mistake

❌ Not using try-catch

## Mini Challenge
Parse args[1] instead

## Recap
Always protect number parsing

lessons.title: Multiple Arguments & Subcommands
lessons.content:
# Multiple Arguments (FULL GUIDE)

## Step 1: Example Command

/give Steve diamond 5

## Step 2: Code

```java
if (args.length < 3) return true;

String playerName = args[0];
String item = args[1];
int amount = Integer.parseInt(args[2]);
```

## Step 3: Breakdown

args[0] → player
args[1] → item
args[2] → amount

## Step 4: Subcommands

```java
if (args[0].equalsIgnoreCase("give")) {

}
```

.equalsIgnoreCase
→ compares text ignoring case

## Step 5: Structure

args[0] = action
args[1+] = data

## Step 6: What Happens

Command splits into parts

## Step 7: Common Mistake

❌ Forgetting length check

## Mini Challenge
Make /heal <player> <amount>

## Recap
Each arg has meaning

quiz.question: What is args[0] often used for?
